coredata.dc:title: Sonochemical doping of Ti-catalyzed sodium aluminum hydride
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Prozorov, Tanya
Wang, Jun
Ebner, Armin D.
Ritter, James A.
coredata.dc:description: A new and very effective sonochemical technique has been developed for doping NaAlH4 with metal catalyst prior to high energy ball milling. When NaAlH4 was sonochemically doped with 2mol% TiCl3 in a decalin slurry using THF as a co-solvent and then ball milled, the dehydrogenation temperature in the 90–150°C range decreased by about 30°C during temperature programmed desorption (5°C/min) compared to a conventionally wet doped and ball milled sample. Similarly, during constant temperature desorption the dehydrogenation kinetics of sonochemically doped and ball milled samples of NaAlH4 increased by factors of 9.0, 5.1 and 3.1, respectively, at 90, 110 and 130°C over those exhibited by conventionally wet doped and ball milled samples. These marked kinetic enhancements persisted through several dehydrogenation/hydrogenation cycles, now with corresponding increases by factors of 16.1, 4.5 and 3.5, and with a striking factor of four improvement in the hydrogenation kinetics also realized at 1250psia and 125°C. The observed kinetic effect was interpreted in terms of the complementary mechanochemical effects imparted to the sample by high-intensity ultrasound followed by high energy ball milling. It was surmised that sonochemical doping induced superior mixing of the titanium(III) chloride and sodium aluminum hydride reagents, thereby fostering the formation of smaller catalyst and NaAlH4 particles. This ensured a finer dispersion of the catalyst particles over the surfaces of the NaAlH4 crystals prior to and during the subsequent high energy ball milling process, which greatly improved the mechanochemical effectiveness of ball milling. Strong evidence in support of this supposition stemmed from sonochemical doping being only marginally more effective than conventional wet doping without subsequent ball milling, and scanning electron microscopy images revealing the formation of the factor of ten smaller NaAlH4 particles for the sonochemically doped and ball milled samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alanates
Hydrogen storage
Sodium aluminum hydride
Titanium chloride
Sonochemical doping
Wet doping
Dehydrogenation and hydrogenation kinetic enhancement
Nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Temperature programmed desorption (TPD) curves obtained at 5°C/min for just prepared (0th cycle discharge) samples of NaAlH4 under the following conditions: two TPD curves for un-doped samples of NaAlH4 irradiated with ultrasound in decalin and then either ball milled or not; four TPD curves for un-ball milled samples of NaAlH4 wet doped, sonochemically doped in decalin, sonochemically doped in decalin with diethyl ether (Et2O), and sonochemically doped in decalin with tetrahydrofuran (THF), all doped with 2mol% Ti; four TPD curves for ball milled samples of NaAlH4 wet doped, sonochemically doped in decalin, sonochemically doped in decalin with Et2O, and sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. Sonochemically doped samples are denoted with ‘)))’; sonochemical irradiation was performed for 120min at 258K; and high energy ball milling was performed for 120min at ambient temperature.

Constant temperature desorption (CTD) curves obtained on just doped (0th cycle discharge) and ball milled samples of NaAlH4 at (A) 90°C, (B) 110°C, and (C) 130°C under the following conditions: wet doped, sonochemically doped in decalin with Et2O, and sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. Sonochemically doped samples are denoted with ‘)))’; sonochemical irradiation was performed for 120min at 258K; and high energy ball milling was performed for 120min at ambient temperature.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images obtained for just doped (0th cycle discharge) and ball milled samples of NaAlH4 under the following conditions: (A) wet doped, (B) sonochemically doped in decalin with Et2O, and (C) sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. Sonochemical irradiation was performed for 120min at 258K; and high energy ball milling was performed for 120min at ambient temperature.

Temperature programmed desorption (TPD) curves obtained at 5°Cmin−1 for just doped (0th cycle discharge), ball milled and cycled (5th cycle discharge) samples of NaAlH4 under the following conditions: wet doped and sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. Sonochemically doped samples are denoted with ‘)))’; sonochemical irradiation was performed for 120min at 258K; and high energy ball milling was performed for 120min at ambient temperature.

Constant temperature desorption (CTD) curves obtained on just doped (0th cycle discharge), ball milled and cycled (5th cycle discharge) samples of NaAlH4 at (A) 90°C, (B) 110°C, and (C) 130°C under the following conditions: wet doped and sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. Sonochemically doped samples are denoted with ‘)))’; sonochemical irradiation was performed for 120min at 258K; and high energy ball milling was performed for 120min at ambient temperature.

Qualitative hydrogenation and dehydrogenation rates obtained during four charge (Po =1250psia) and four discharge (P o =15psia) cycles carried out at 125°C for ball milled samples of NaAlH4 wet doped and sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. The filled symbols correspond to the sonochemically doped sample; the empty symbols correspond to the wet doped sample.

article.ce:title: Sonochemical doping of Ti-catalyzed sodium aluminum hydride

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Swearingen Engineering Center, University of South Carolina, Columbia, South Carolina 29208, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new and very effective sonochemical technique has been developed for doping NaAlH4 with metal catalyst prior to high energy ball milling. When NaAlH4 was sonochemically doped with 2mol% TiCl3 in a decalin slurry using THF as a co-solvent and then ball milled, the dehydrogenation temperature in the 90–150°C range decreased by about 30°C during temperature programmed desorption (5°C/min) compared to a conventionally wet doped and ball milled sample. Similarly, during constant temperature desorption the dehydrogenation kinetics of sonochemically doped and ball milled samples of NaAlH4 increased by factors of 9.0, 5.1 and 3.1, respectively, at 90, 110 and 130°C over those exhibited by conventionally wet doped and ball milled samples. These marked kinetic enhancements persisted through several dehydrogenation/hydrogenation cycles, now with corresponding increases by factors of 16.1, 4.5 and 3.5, and with a striking factor of four improvement in the hydrogenation kinetics also realized at 1250psia and 125°C. The observed kinetic effect was interpreted in terms of the complementary mechanochemical effects imparted to the sample by high-intensity ultrasound followed by high energy ball milling. It was surmised that sonochemical doping induced superior mixing of the titanium(III) chloride and sodium aluminum hydride reagents, thereby fostering the formation of smaller catalyst and NaAlH4 particles. This ensured a finer dispersion of the catalyst particles over the surfaces of the NaAlH4 crystals prior to and during the subsequent high energy ball milling process, which greatly improved the mechanochemical effectiveness of ball milling. Strong evidence in support of this supposition stemmed from sonochemical doping being only marginally more effective than conventional wet doping without subsequent ball milling, and scanning electron microscopy images revealing the formation of the factor of ten smaller NaAlH4 particles for the sonochemically doped and ball milled samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The complex hydride, sodium aluminum hydride (NaAlH4) is part of a new class of reversible hydrogen storage materials referred to as alanates. When doped with a Ti catalyst [1–5], this material discharges reasonable amounts of hydrogen at atmospheric pressure at about 80°C lower than un-doped NaAlH4 [6]. More importantly, the Ti catalyst also facilitates a kinetic pathway for reversibility, with reasonable charge rates achieved at around 125°C and 1200psia [3,7–10]. Although this material has a theoretical potential to reversibly store 5.6wt% hydrogen, numerous studies have shown that the state-of-the-art is around 3wt% reversible hydrogen storage at reasonable temperatures, pressures, and discharge and charge rates [3,6–10].

A 3-wt% reversible hydrogen storage capacity is currently below that being sought for most transportation applications [3]. Nevertheless, this alanate compound serves as a model complex hydride material, with understanding of the catalytic effect and ways to improve its kinetics continually being sought. Hence, any improvements in the dehydrogenation and hydrogenation kinetics of this metal-doped NaAlH4 system, which necessarily translates into higher kinetic rates at lower temperatures and charge pressures, might bring this or other complex hydride materials closer to commercial applications.

The first major improvement in the dehydrogenation and hydrogenation kinetics of this metal-doped NaAlH4 system, after that reported by Bogdanovic and Schwickardi [6] based on a simple wet doping procedure, was achieved through the use of a high energy ball milling doping procedure as first proposed by Jenson and co-workers [5]. High energy ball milling has since been used in essentially all studies of metal catalyzed alanates, emerging as the preferred doping technique [2,5,7–20]. Further dehydrogenation and hydrogenation kinetic enhancements have been achieved through the use of co-metal dopants such as Fe and Zr [21,22] with Ti [9,10,22,23], with Ti being the best single metal catalyst when compared to literally dozens of different metal catalysts [5,10,13,20,23]. Faster discharge and charge kinetics have also been achieved through the use of graphite as a co-dopant with Ti, as first shown by Wang et al. [20] and then by Dehouche et al [24]. One common factor that stands out among these more recent studies is that the reported enhancements simply augmented the results obtained by high energy ball milling, and thus depended on its use [5,15–17,25–31].

High energy ball milling utilizes a direct transfer of mechanical energy from a metal or ceramic ball to a material that it comes in contact with through high energy collisions. These collisions create intense localized stresses and strains that induce structural changes and chemical reactions within the material, even at ambient temperature [32–37]. For example, structural changes associated with repeated fracturing and cold welding of a material has lead to highly metastable amorphous alloys and nanostructured intermetallic compounds [31,32,34,35,38–45]. In addition, collision-induced chemical reactions has improved the hydrogenation properties of several hydrogen absorbing alloys [46] or even facilitated the low-temperature formation of novel hydrides with otherwise unstable alloy phases through reactive ball milling in a hydrogen atmosphere [47,48]. The similarities between high energy ball milling induced mechanochemical effects and those produced from the irradiation of solid-liquid suspensions (or slurries) with high intensity ultrasonic waves are striking [49–55]. Hence, it is surprising that high intensity ultrasound has not been investigated for the sonochemical doping of NaAlH4 with metal catalyst.

Ultrasonically-induced acoustic cavitation produces intense turbulent flow and shock waves, which result in extremely rapid interfacial mass transfer [56–59]. The shock waves also foster high-velocity collisions between suspended particles, almost instantaneously creating extremely high temperatures and subsequent fast cooling rates localized at the point of impact [56,60–62]. The estimated speed of the colliding particles approaches half the speed of sound in the liquid, with the resulting high-velocity collisions causing morphological changes in a variety of materials, including structural as well as chemical changes [56,60–62]. Clearly, high intensity ultrasound and high energy ball milling should exhibit similar or complementary effects during the processing of materials, like in the processing of Ti-doped NaAlH4.

Therefore, the objective of this work is to report for the first time the use of heterogeneous sonochemistry as an effective means of doping NaAlH4 with Ti catalyst. It is shown that when high intensity ultrasound is used prior to high energy ball milling to irradiate a decalin slurry containing a co-solvent, such as tetrahydrofuran or diethyl ether, and a mixture of sodium aluminum hydride and titanium(III) chloride, the resulting sonochemically doped and ball milled sample exhibits a striking enhancement in both the dehydrogenation and hydrogenation kinetics, persisting even after several hydrogenation/dehydrogenation cycles. A series of temperature programmed desorption (TPD) and constant temperature desorption (CTD) experiments are presented in this preliminary work to clearly reveal the pronounced effects of this new sonochemical doping technique when combined with high energy ball milling. Also, a plausible explanation is offered as to the cause of the observed kinetic enhancements associated with the complementary use of the new heterogeneous sonochemical doping technique with high energy ball milling.

Decanydronaphthalene (decalin) (Aldrich, 98% cis + trans mixture, anhydrous) was distilled over NaAlH4 and degassed under dry argon flow for several hours. Pentane (Aldrich, 99.5%, anhydrous) was distilled over NaAlH4 under argon. TiCl3 (Aldrich, 99.99%, anhydrous), tetrahydrofuran (THF) (Aldrich, 99.5%, anhydrous), dodecane (Aldrich, 99.5%, anhydrous), and diethyl ether (Aldrich, 99.5%, anhydrous) were all used as received. NaAlH4 powder (Fluka, 99.5%) was recrystallized from a 3M THF solution (Aldrich, 99.9%, anhydrous), filtered through 0.7μm filter paper, and vacuum-dried. All sample handling procedures were performed in a nitrogen-filled glove box.

A conventional wet doping procedure was performed by mixing 1.007±0.002g (18.64±0.1mmol) of NaAlH4 with 0.057g (3.80×10−4 mol) of TiCl3 in 10mL of THF to produce a sample doped with 2mol% titanium; the molar content of the catalyst was based on the Na or Al content in NaAlH4. NaAlH4 and the catalyst were then mixed using a mortar and pestle for about 30min, or until the material appeared to be completely dry; it was then subjected to high energy ball milling.

A new sonochemical doping procedure was developed and carried out in a 50mL custom sonochemical flask, where 1.007±0.002g (18.64±0.1mmol) of NaAlH4 were suspended in a mixture of 40mL of decahydranapthalene and 10mL of THF. To this suspension, 0.059g (3.82×10−4 mol) of TiCl3 were added to produce doped samples containing 2mol% titanium. The flask was sealed, taken out of the glovebox and briefly sparged with dry argon. Sonochemical doping of the samples was performed for 120min at 258K by using a direct immersion ultrasonic horn (Sonics VCX-750 at 20kHz and ∼50W/cm2) under moderate argon flow (30mL/min). The resulting ultrasonically treated powder was transferred to the glovebox, collected by filtration on a fritted glass funnel, repeatedly washed with dry pentane (6×30mL), and dried overnight at room temperature.

High energy ball milling of the dry powders was carried out for 120min using a SPEX 8000 high energy ball mill. This mill was loaded with a 65cm3 stainless steel (SS) vial containing approximately 1g of powder and a single SS ball (8.2g) with a diameter of 1.3cm. High energy ball milling was also carried out in a nitrogen filled glove box.

Thermogravimetric analysis was carried out with a Perkin Elmer, TGA 7 Series thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA). The dehydrogenation rates of all NaAlH4 samples were measured at atmospheric pressure under helium flow (∼60mL/min) in temperature programmed desorption (TPD) and constant temperature desorption (CTD) modes. For TPD runs, the samples were heated to 250°C at a ramping rate of 5°C/min after purging with helium for 1min. For CTD runs, a similar procedure was followed except that the samples were heated rapidly to the destination temperature and held at that temperature for a predetermined time. Approximately 10mg of sample were used in each TPD and CTD measurement.

A 3000psia Parr reactor, installed in an automated pressure and temperature cycling system, was used to evaluate the dehydrogenation and hydrogenation kinetics of various doped and ball milled samples of NaAlH4 during and after cycling. The reactor conditions were continuously monitored and controlled with a computer. A sample of doped and ball milled alanate was loaded into the reactor vessel inside the nitrogen filled glove box. The reactor was then sealed, removed from the glove box and connected to the automated cycling system.

A typical sequence of dehydrogenation/hydrogenation cycles was carried out by first discharging a sample by heating the reactor to 125°C and holding it at this temperature for 120min. During this time, the pressure in the sealed vessel increased from 15psia to a slightly higher pressure, indicating dehydrogenation had occurred. This is referred to as the 0th cycle discharge. Then, a sample was hydrogenated (charged) by pressurizing the reactor with hydrogen (National Welders, HP Grade) to 1250psia while maintaining the temperature at 125°C and holding it at this temperature for 75min. During this time, the pressure in the sealed vessel decreased from 1250psia to a slightly lower pressure, indicating hydrogenation had occurred. This step is referred to as the 1st cycle charge. The corresponding 1st cycle discharge was carried out by depressurizing the reactor to about 20psia while maintaining the temperature at 125°C and holding it this temperature for 120min. The cycling procedure was repeated several times in this manner. After completing the 5th charge cycle, the sample was maintained at 1250psia to prevent dehydrogenation while the temperature was reduced to room temperature (∼25°C). Then, the pressure was decreased to atmospheric pressure (∼15psia) and the sealed reactor was transferred to the glove box to remove the sample for subsequent TPD and CTD studies.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of all samples before and after ball milling were obtained using an ESEM FEI Quanta 200 scanning electron microscope equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, which was used for determining the chemical composition of some of the samples. Again, all of these air and moisture sensitive NaAlH4 samples were prepared for SEM examination in the nitrogen filled glove box by loading them on two-sided carbon tape, mounting them on sample holders, coating them with several drops of a 50:50 (v/v) mixture of pentane and dodecane to ensure formation of a protective liquid hydrocarbon layer, and sealing them in double-walled individual screw-top containers. The carbonaceous layer essentially protected these samples while they were unavoidably, but only briefly, exposed to ambient air when mounting the sample holders in the Au-sputtering device prior to degassing, as described elsewhere [20].

A series of TPD and CTD experiments were carried out to clearly reveal the effect of the sonochemical doping technique on the dehydrogenation and hydrogenation kinetics of Ti-catalyzed NaAlH4. Fig. 1 displays three sets of TPD curves obtained from just prepared, un-cycled samples of NaAlH4. The first set consists of two TPD curves for un-doped samples of NaAlH4 irradiated with ultrasound in decalin and then either ball milled or not. The first point to emphasize from these two TPD curves is that the ultrasonic horn did not provide any metal catalyst to the NaAlH4 during the sonication process. Very high dehydrogenation temperatures were exhibited by these samples whether ball milled or not, with some improvement noted after ball milling. These results corroborate the findings obtained from EDX analyses of these same samples (not shown), which did not reveal the presence of titanium or any other impurities that may have originated from the abrasion of the direct-immersion Ti horn during sonication.

The next point to make is that the effect of sonochemical doping is not very effective without subsequent high energy ball milling of the sample. This point is made with the second set of curves that consist of four TPD curves for un-ball milled samples of NaAlH4 wet doped, sonochemically doped in decalin, sonochemically doped in decalin with diethyl ether (Et2O), and sonochemically doped in decalin with tetrahydrofuran (THF), all doped with 2mol% Ti. These curves all fell within 15°C of each other with significant dehydrogenation not occurring until about 160°C and then persisting up to about 190°C in all three cases. In the 170°C range, the lowest dehydrogenation temperature was obtained with the sample sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, followed by that sonochemically doped in decalin with Et2O. The samples sonochemically doped in decalin and wet doped both exhibited higher dehydrogenation temperatures, with similar behaviors persisting once the melting point of NaAlH4 was reached at about 180°C. Clearly, sonochemical doping without a co-solvent present was essentially equivalent to wet doping when neither doping technique was combined with high energy ball milling. The positive effect of the co-solvent is explained later.

The final and most important point to emphasize is that the effect of sonochemical doping can be very effective when combined with subsequent high energy ball milling of the sample. This point is made with the third set of curves that consist of four TPD curves for ball milled samples of NaAlH4 wet doped, sonochemically doped in decalin, sonochemically doped in decalin with Et2O, and sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. These curves all exhibited significantly lower dehydrogenation temperatures than the doped but not ball milled samples. The lowest dehydrogenation temperature was obtained with the sample sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, which was 30°C lower than the wet doped and ball milled sample and 45°C lower that its un-ball milled counterpart, both striking effects. The next lowest dehydrogenation temperature was exhibited by the sample sonochemically doped in decalin with Et2O, which was only 5°C lower than the wet doped and ball milled sample, but still 35°C lower that its un-ball milled counterpart. The third lowest dehydrogenation temperature was obtained from the wet doped and ball milled sample, with a dehydrogenation temperature 38°C lower than its un-ball milled counterpart. Finally, the sample sonochemically doped in just decalin did not fare as well as the wet doped sample, exhibiting an 8°C higher dehydrogenation temperature; but it was still 28°C lower that its un-ball milled counterpart. This last result was most likely due to the limited production of catalyst during sonication, as NaAlH4 and TiCl3, which must react together to produce the catalyst (see below), are both insoluble in decalin. Wet doping performed better in this regard, because NaAlH4 is at least soluble in THF, although TiCl3 is not. This reactant solubility issue with respect to sonochemical doping is explained in more detail later. Clearly, sonochemical doping when combined with high energy ball milling produced an effect that was far greater than that associated with just high energy ball milling alone, especially when the proper co-solvent was used. An explanation for this complementary effect of high intensity ultrasound when combined with high energy ball milling is provided later.

The TDP curves shown in Fig. 1 prove without a doubt that the combined use of sonochemical doping and high energy ball milling produced a material with enhanced dehydrogenation kinetics. The complementary effects of these high energy materials processing steps are further substantiated by the independently obtained CTD curves provided in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2 compares three sets of CTD curves with each set corresponding to a different temperature, i.e., 90°C (Fig. 2A), 110°C (Fig. 2B), and 130°C (Fig. 2C). The three curves within each set consist of CTD curves for ball milled samples of NaAlH4 wet doped, sonochemically doped in decalin with Et2O, and sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. Noticeably improved dehydrogenation kinetics were observed at all three temperatures for both of the sonochemically doped NaAlH4 samples over those of the wet doped sample. The sample sonochemically doped in decalin with THF consistently exhibited the best behavior, with the most pronounced effects observed at 90°C, followed by those at 110°C, and then by those at 130°C. For example, at 90, 110, and 130°C, the desorption rates displayed by the sample sonochemically doped in decalin with THF were, respectively, 9.0, 5.1 and 3.1 times faster than those of the wet doped sample.

An analysis of the amount of hydrogen released during the very linear regions of the CTD curves shown in Fig. 2 was consistent with that corresponding only to the first dehydrogenation reaction, as discussed elsewhere [20,63]. In Fig. 2A, the sample sonochemically doped in decalin with THF released approximately 2.0wt% hydrogen from the first decomposition reaction over 80min. Although 2.0wt% hydrogen was below the theoretical limit of the first reaction (2.9wt%) [63], the corresponding discharge rate of 1.8wt% H2/h significantly surpassed any rate reported to date for NaAlH4 doped with as little as 2mol% TiCl3 at 90°C [2,5,8,11,16–19,64]. In contrast, the sample sonochemically doped in decalin with Et2O exhibited far slower dehydrogenation kinetics, releasing 0.64wt% H2/h; and the wet doped sample released the least amount of hydrogen at an exceedingly slow rate of 0.22wt% H2/h, with this latter rate being essentially state-of-the-art at 90°C for a ball milled sample of NaAlH4 containing 2mol% Ti as the catalyst. Similarly fast hydrogen discharge rates of 7.4 and 28.7wt% H2/h were exhibited, respectively, at 110 and 130°C by the samples sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, compared to 1.4 (110°C) and 8.6 (130°C) wt% H2/h by the samples wet doped, which again represent the state-of-the-art at these temperatures and Ti doping levels.

It is clear from the results presented in Figs. 1 and 2 that sonochemical doping without ball milling is only marginally better than wet doping without ball milling. It is also clear that sonochemical doping with ball milling causes a marked enhancement in the dehydrogenation kinetics beyond that which could be obtained simply by ball milling alone. At the outset it was surmised that sonochemical doping induced superior mixing of the titanium(III) chloride and sodium aluminum hydride reagents, thereby fostering the formation of smaller catalyst and NaAlH4 particles. This ensured a finer dispersion of the catalyst particles over the surfaces of the NaAlH4 crystals prior to and during the subsequent high energy ball milling process, which greatly improved the mechanochemical effectiveness of ball milling. Strong evidence in support of this supposition stems not only from sonochemical doping being only slightly more effective than conventional wet doping without subsequent ball milling (Fig. 1), but also from scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images revealing the formation of the smaller NaAlH4 particles for the sonochemically doped and ball milled samples. These SEM images are shown in Fig. 3 .

Fig. 3 displays three SEM images of ball milled samples of NaAlH4 (A) wet doped, (B) sonochemically doped in decalin with Et2O, and (C) sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. In general, the rather large agglomerates of particles from the wet doped sample that are visible in Fig. 3A were typically much larger than 20μm in diameter. These particles were greatly reduced in size to less than 10μm in diameter for the sample sonochemically doped in decalin with Et2O (Fig. 3B), and to much less than 2μm in diameter for the sample sonochemically doped in decalin with THF (Fig. 3C). In the latter case, agglomerates of particles were conspicuously absent from any of the SEM images taken of this material. These results corroborate the CTD curves shown in Fig. 2, where the dehydrogenation kinetics increased in the same order as the particle sizes decreased in Fig. 3. They also agree with the first set of TPD curves discussed with reference to Fig. 1. Clearly, sonochemical irradiation of decalin slurries of NaAlH4 led to a significant reduction of particle size. However, it was also suspected that sonochemical doping produced very finely divided Ti catalyst particles, which allowed them to be more greatly dispersed over the NaAlH4 particles. This improved the effectiveness of the high energy ball milling process, especially since the surface area of the NaAlH4 particles had substantially increased due to their smaller sizes (Fig. 3). It was further suspected that the catalyst particle size was related, in part, to the solubility of the reagents in the sonochemical slurry, as explained below.

It was surmised that for sonochemical doping to be effective, the solvent or co-solvents had to allow the catalyst species to form by fostering a reaction between the catalyst precursor TiCl3 and the alanate NaAlH4 [65,66]. Decalin, a common sonochemical solvent, was initially selected as the primary sonication solvent due to its high boiling point and thus low vapor pressure. However, due to their negligible solubility in alkanes [67], neither NaAlH4 nor TiCl3 dissociated in decalin. The use of decalin apparently limited the extent of the chemical reaction that took place between these two suspended powders to the solid-solid interface created during ultrasound-induced interparticle collisions in the decalin slurry, with the liquid phase presumably further dampening these interparticle collisions. No anticipated color change or evolution of hydrogen gas was observed when the initial slurry containing the reagents was irradiated with ultrasound, which perhaps indicated that the reaction between NaAlH4 and TiCl3 was necessarily incomplete. The greatly impeded, high temperature dehydrogenation kinetics exhibited by the TPD curves shown in Fig. 1 for the samples of NaAlH4 sonochemically doped in decalin without a co-solvent, whether ball milled or not, exemplify these points very well. When a co-solvent with the capability of dissolving NaAlH4 was added to the decalin, the results dramatically improved, however. In fact, this was the reasoning behind choosing THF as a co-solvent.

When a small amount of THF was added to decalin, a two-phase liquid system was immediately formed, with ultrasound rapidly producing a fine emulsion of the co-solvent (dispersed phase) in decalin (continuous phase). NaAlH4 dissolved in the finely dispersed phase, while TiCl3 remained in solid form in either phase due to it essentially being insoluble in both THF and decalin [67]. Nevertheless, the newly formed interfacial area between the solid (TiCl3) and liquid (NaAlH4 dissolved in THF) phases presumably created numerous sites and a liquid phase for the chemical reaction to take place between these two reagents. In fact, during the first 5 min of ultrasonic irradiation, evolution of hydrogen gas was observed and the initial pale purple suspension darkened and turned black, implying that the reaction between the reagents had occurred and was most likely carried out to completion. The reaction product intermediate Ti(AlH4)3 that was suspected of being formed in the THF, although with undetermined solubility in THF, was thermally unstable at the reaction conditions employed here (−10°C). Hence, it readily decomposed forming a precipitate (most likely composed of the yet to be identified catalyst species), that settled out of the finely distributed dispersed phase, potentially suppressing agglomeration of the catalyst particles. This supposition also explains why THF was a better co-solvent than Et2O, even though Et2O also formed a two-phase emulsion when added to decalin and sonicated. It is noteworthy that Et2O was selected as an alternative co-solvent because it was presumed that the suspected reaction product intermediate Ti(AlH4)3 was soluble in it [65,68,69], and that NaAlH4 may exhibit limited solubility in Et2O [65,70,71].

With NaAlH4 reported to be essentially insoluble [68,69,72–75] or at best only slightly soluble in Et2O [67,71], its use as a co-solvent was limited to exhibiting a behavior somewhere in between that observed with THF in decalin and just decalin with no co-solvent added, as shown in Fig. 1. There are two plausible explanations as to why sonochemical doping in decalin with Et2O showed any improvement at all in the dehydrogenation kinetics. If NaAlH4 was the least bit soluble in Et2O, then it reacted with TiCl3 during sonication in the same way that it did in THF; however, the reaction most likely proceeded much more slowly due to NaAlH4 molecules having to continuously dissolve in the dispersed phase as the insoluble reaction product was formed and precipitated out of this phase. Based on this argument, Et2O has the potential to be as good as THF as a co-solvent when given enough time for this solubility limited reaction to proceed during sonochemical doping (the topic of future work). Even if NaAlH4 was completely insoluble in Et2O, another possibility existed to explain its behavior, based on the similarity with this work and several reports on other metal aluminum hydrides, such as Be(AlH4)2 [65], Mg(AlH4)2 [70,76], Fe(AlH4)2 [77], Ti(AlH4)4 [68,69], and Nb(AlH4)3.5 [78]. It was surmised that the reaction between NaAlH4 and TiCl3 yielded a thermally unstable reaction product intermediate Ti(AlH4)3 that was soluble in Et2O, which drove the reaction by slowing consuming the insoluble products at the high surface area liquid–liquid–solid interface. However, since Ti(AlH4)3 was presumed to be extremely unstable at the temperatures used in the sonochemical doping experiments (−10°C), similarly to the works of Wiberg and co-workers for the case of Ti(AlH4)4 [68,69], the decomposition product once again precipitated from the dispersed phase, further driving the reaction by forming the yet to be identified active catalyst species containing Ti, as also occurred in THF. In either one of these scenarios, the net result was that the sonochemically driven reaction pathway compared to the wet doped driven one, suppressed the agglomeration of the catalyst particles, resulting in smaller catalyst particles to be dispersed over the surfaces of the NaAlH4 particles prior to and during the subsequent high energy ball milling process.

All of the results displayed and discussed so far in Figs. 1–3 correspond to the 0th cycle discharge, i.e., to the behavior of freshly doped, ball milled and discharged samples of un-cycled NaAlH4. The obvious questions that remain are whether the effects of sonochemical doping persist after several dehydrogenation/hydrogenation cycles are carried out, and whether the improved kinetic effects are exhibited during hydrogenation. Fig. 4 compares two sets of TPD curves obtained for ball milled and cycled samples of NaAlH4 wet doped and sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. The first set consists of TPD curves of doped and ball milled samples of NaAlH4 obtained before cycling (i.e., 0th discharge); and the second set consists of TPD curves of doped and ball milled samples of NaAlH4 obtained after cycling five times (i.e., 5th discharge). These TPD curves show very clearly that even after five dehydrogenation/hydrogenation cycles were carried out, the sonochemically doped sample consistently exhibited dehydrogenation temperatures of at least 20°C below that of the wet doped sample. In both cases, cycling caused an increase in the dehydrogenation temperature, but less so for the sonochemically doped sample; and in both cases, cycling caused noticeable decreases in the overall hydrogen discharge capacity by about 0.75wt%, which is typical for samples prepared via the wet doping technique [9–11,20]. Hence, the positive effects of sonochemical doping persisted through cycling as further revealed from the CTD curves shown in Fig. 5 .

Fig. 5 compares three sets of CTD curves with each set corresponding to a different temperature (i.e., 90, 110 and 130°C). The four curves within each set consist of CTD curves before cycling (i.e., 0th discharge) and after cycling five times (i.e., 5th discharge) for ball milled samples of NaAlH4 wet doped and sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. Although the dehydrogenation rates of both the sonochemical and wet doped samples decreased after five dehydrogenation/hydrogenation cycles were carried out, the discharge kinetics were once again strikingly better for the sonochemically doped samples at all three temperatures. For example, sonochemical doping over wet doping improved the dehydrogenation kinetics by factors of 16.1, 5.1 and 3.1 at 90, 110, 130°C, respectively, even after cycling. Note the pronounced increase in this ratio at 90°C from 9.0 before cycling to 16.1 after cycling. Moreover, although somewhat diminished compared to the un-cycled samples, the hydrogen discharge rates of these sonochemically doped and cycled samples were still quite respectable, with them producing 0.96, 5.1 and 17.8wt% H2/h at 90, 110, 130°C, respectively. In contrast, the wet doped and cycled sample at 90°C released a barely detectable amount of hydrogen at a rate of only 0.06wt% H2/h. The hydrogen discharged from the wet doped and cycled samples at 110 and 130°C were certainly detectable, but the corresponding rates were far below those of the sonochemically doped samples at 1.4 and 4.6wt% H2/h, respectively.

The results just presented and discussed show that the very favorable effects of sonochemical doping on the dehydrogenation performance of Ti-doped NaAlH4 persisted even after cycling. The results in Fig. 6 show a similarly positive effect of sonochemical doping on the hydrogenation kinetics of Ti-doped NaAlH4 even after cycling. This figure displays qualitative hydrogenation and dehydrogenation rates obtained during four charge (P o =1250psia) and four discharge (P o =15psia) cycles carried out at 125°C with doped and ball milled samples of NaAlH4 wet doped and sonochemically doped in decalin with THF, all doped with 2mol% Ti. The qualitative kinetics of charge and discharge are inferred from the observed linear decreases and increases in pressure over time. The distinct influence of sonochemical doping not only on the dehydrogenation (discharge) kinetics, but also on the hydrogenation (charge) kinetics, is clearly observed. In fact, the time for charging was markedly decreased by a factor of four, from about 60min to about 15min. These results may represent the best charge kinetics to date for a sample of NaAlH4 doped with as little as 2mol% Ti and are comparable to those reported recently when graphite was used as a co-dopant with Ti [20]. Similarly, the discharge kinetics increased by a factor of three, from about 90min to about 30min, which agrees quite well with the increase in the discharge rate at 130°C observed from the CTD curves in Fig. 5C. It was also interesting that after the first cycle, the sonochemically doped sample cycled more consistently than the wet doped sample, attaining periodic behavior after just one cycle. The charge and discharge profiles for the wet doped sample did not exhibit periodic behavior even after four cycles, especially the discharge profiles. Hence, it appears that sonochemically doped samples over their wet doped counterparts not only exhibit marked improvements in both the charge and discharge kinetics, but they also cycle more consistently.

Finally, a brief discussion is provided to highlight the differences between high intensity ultrasound and high energy ball milling that foster the complementary behavior observed during the processing of Ti-catalyzed NaAlH4. It was apparent that high energy ball milling played the key role in the mechanochemical activation of the catalyst species within the NaAlH4 structure. Such activation most likely reflected the efficacy of ball milling in bringing the catalyst particles into intimate contact with the surfaces of the NaAlH4 particles, due to its innate ability to de-agglomerate, homogenize and disperse the catalyst particles over the surface of the material being ball milled through intense collisions between the material and the milling bead. During these collisions, all the energy released from the stainless steel ball was transferred directly into the material being ball milled. In contrast, since acoustic energy is inherently mechanical and since ultrasonic wavelengths are several orders of magnitude larger than molecular dimensions, acoustic energy was not directly absorbed by the dissolved species or suspended solid particles in the sonicated slurry. Instead, the chemical effects of ultrasound were derived primarily from acoustic cavitation, i.e., the formation, growth, and implosive violent collapse of bubbles [62,79,80]. Moreover, whereas high energy ball milling was operated with powdery dry solids to provide direct contact with the milling bead, the ultrasound-driven interparticle collisions required a liquid medium (normally, a high-boiling alkane) for the waves to travel through. Hence, only a fraction of the acoustic energy released into the liquid was utilized to induce sonochemical reactions between the catalyst precursor solids and the dissolved NaAlH4 molecules dispersed in the sonicated slurry. Therefore, in terms of utilization of the mechanical energy and impact-driven homogenization on contact, heterogeneous sonochemistry appeared to be less effective than ball milling. However, in terms of sonochemical-induced particle fracturing, heterogeneous sonochemistry appeared to be more effective than high energy ball milling most likely due to acoustic cavitation. In addition, reaction times during ball milling are a few orders of magnitude longer than cavitation timescales during sonication, leading to significantly slower cooling rates possibly resulting in more heat-induced structural changes during ball milling. These differences between high intensity ultrasound and high energy ball milling became quite evident during the course of this study, with the complementary effects being readily explained in terms of sonochemical-induced fracturing of both the catalyst and NaAlH4 particles which subsequently improved the mechanochemical effectiveness of high energy ball milling.

With these thoughts in mind, although the exact role of high intensity ultrasound remains unclear, it was assumed that the presence of the liquid reduced agglomeration of the suspended and heavily agitated solid particles, and that it facilitated their fragmentation while constantly being redispersed into the surrounding slurry. It appeared that the reduction of particle agglomeration in this case was significantly more effective than that achieved during ball milling alone, with shear stresses induced by ultrasound apparently playing a significant role. Once the material was sonochemically doped and dried, ball milling finished the doping task by directly activating the catalyst fragments already thoroughly dispersed over the alanate surface at the point of impact. To some extent, powder fragmentation may have still occurred during ball milling, but it was suspected that the dispersion of the highly fractionated catalyst particles rapidly ceased as they became deeply immersed into the surface of the NaAlH4 particle upon a few impacts with the milling bead. As a consequence, catalyst dispersion over the surface was not as effective during high energy ball milling unless the samples were previously doped sonochemically; and conversely, catalyst and NaAlH4 particle fracturing during sonochemical doping was not as effective unless the samples were subsequently ball milled.

This study reported on the first time use of heterogeneous sonochemistry as an effective means of doping NaAlH4 with Ti catalyst. It was shown that when high intensity ultrasound was used prior to high energy ball milling to irradiate a decalin slurry containing a co-solvent such as tetrahydrofuran or diethyl ether and a mixture of sodium aluminum hydride and titanium(III) chloride, the resulting sonochemically doped and ball milled sample exhibited a striking enhancement in both the dehydrogenation and hydrogenation kinetics, which persisted even after several hydrogenation/dehydrogenation cycles. A series of temperature programmed desorption (TPD) and constant temperature desorption (CTD) experiments clearly revealed the pronounced effects of this new sonochemical doping technique when combined with high energy ball milling. Also, an explanation was offered as to the cause of the observed kinetic enhancements associated with the complementary use of the new heterogeneous sonochemical doping technique with high energy ball milling.

For example, when NaAlH4 was sonochemically doped with 2mol% TiCl3 in a decalin slurry using tetrahydrofuran as a co-solvent and then ball milled, the dehydrogenation temperature in the 90 to 150°C range decreased by about 30°C during TPD compared to a conventionally wet doped and ball milled sample. Similarly, during CTD the dehydrogenation kinetics of sonochemically doped and ball milled samples of NaAlH4 increased by factors of 9.0, 5.1 and 3.1 respectively at 90, 110 and 130°C over those exhibited by conventionally wet doped and ball milled samples. These marked kinetic enhancements persisted through several dehydrogenation/hydrogenation cycles, with the dehydrogenation kinetics of sonochemically doped, ball milled and cycled samples of NaAlH4 still increasing by factors of 16.1, 4.5 and 3.5 respectively at 90, 110 and 130°C over those exhibited by conventionally wet doped, ball milled and cycled samples; and for the same materials, a striking factor of four improvement in the hydrogenation kinetics was realized at 1250psia and 125°C. Overall, these results may represent the best discharge and charge kinetics to date for a sample of NaAlH4 doped with as little as 2mol% Ti.

The observed kinetic effect was interpreted in terms of the complementary mechanochemical effects imparted to the sample by high intensity ultrasound followed by high energy ball milling. It was surmised that sonochemical doping induced superior mixing of the titanium(III) chloride and sodium aluminum hydride reagents, thereby fostering the formation of smaller catalyst and NaAlH4 particles. This ensured a finer dispersion of the catalyst particles over the surfaces of the NaAlH4 crystals prior to and during the subsequent high energy ball milling process, which greatly improved the mechanochemical effectiveness of ball milling. Strong evidence in support of this supposition stemmed from sonochemical doping being only marginally more effective than conventional wet doping without subsequent ball milling, and scanning electron microscopy images revealing the formation of the smaller NaAlH4 particles for the sonochemically doped and ball milled samples. In fact, it was inferred from these results that catalyst particle dispersion over the surface of NaAlH4 crystals was not as effective during high energy ball milling unless the samples were previously doped sonochemically; and conversely, catalyst and NaAlH4 particle fracturing during sonochemical doping was not as effective unless the samples were subsequently ball milled.

The ultimate long-term goal of this research is to replace high energy ball milling with high intensity ultrasound, and make it the only necessary step in the doping of complex hydrides with metal catalysts. As a means to this end, a detailed study is underway to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the effects of high intensity ultrasound on the doping of NaAlH4 and other complex hydrides with metal catalysts and co-dopants. Although this goal has yet to be achieved, because the research described in this paper indicates that the sonochemical doping of complex hydrides with metal catalysts must be done in conjunction with high energy ball milling to be effective, the outcome of this work still provides a new, extremely successful, and potentially scalable avenue for the doping of complex hydrides with metal catalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support provided by MeadWestvaco and the Separations Research Program at the University of Texas at Austin, and by Cooperative Agreement DE-FC36-04GO14232 with the DOE was greatly appreciated. Use of the Hitachi S-4700 Field Emission SEM at the University of Illinois Center for Microanalysis of Materials, which is partially supported by the U.S. DOE under grant DEFG02-91-ER45439, was also greatly appreciated.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proc. Int. Conf. Solid–Solid Phase Transform. Inorg. Mater.

D.R. Lide, CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 83rd ed., CRC Press, Inc., Boca Raton, 2002, p. 2500+.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasound: Its Chemical, Physical and Biological Effects
Phil. Trans. R. Soc. Lond. A
